title: Clean Fuels Weekly Commentary: CA LCFS Type 1 Prices Witness Slight Increase as Weekly Volumes Drop
content:
This Weekâ€™s Highlights:

(14th April â€“ 20th April)
  • A total of 315,374 credits were exchanged as Type 1 trades, a fall of 12.2% week-over-week (WoW), while all non-zero trades also witnessed a fall of 11.3% WoW as 2,226,528 credits were traded in CA LCFS. Trading volumes have already reached 7.53 MMT this month, recording the highest volume of trades for a month ever recorded in the Programâ€™s history.
  • The weekly average CA LCFS credit price stood at $58.14/MT for Type 1 transfers, recording a 0.21% rise WoW; All Non-Zero transfers fell by 3.72% in price, reaching $63.14/MT.
  • The average LCFS spot credit price fell by 1.39% WoW; on 22nd April, the spot price stood at $56.25/MT, and the benchmark price stood at $60.75/MT.
  • D3 RIN prices witnessed a fall 1.19% WoW. D4, D5 and D6 RIN prices increased by 1.04% WoW each.

cCarbonâ€™s Market View

On 21st April 2025, CARBâ€™s Third 15-day comment period on the latest proposed amendments to the LCFS concluded. CARB received 81 comments from biofuel groups, OEMs, charging and refueling infrastructure providers, and other stakeholders. On 4th April, 2025, CARB had released its Third 15-Day Package of LCFS amendments, addressing clarity and consistency issues flagged by the Office of Administrative Law. cCarbon will soon publish a comprehensive analysis of the comments received. Several Senators and biofuel groups continue to urge the EPA to set ambitious Renewable Volume Obligations (RVO) for the coming years, while accounting for Small Refinery Exemptions (SRE). D4 and D5 prices reached a high of $1 on 17th April, a price last witnessed in October 2023. The recent rally in RIN prices has been driven by rising soybean feedstock prices, anticipation around RVOs and falling heating oil prices. cCarbon explored the different macroeconomic, federal and state-level factors that would shape the California LCFS and RFS markets in the medium term in a recent webinar. CA LCFS Weekly Price and Volume Trend [caption id="attachment_595666" align="alignnone" width="1000"] Fig 1. Weekly average credit prices and weekly volumes, Source: California ARB[/caption] The average Type 1 prices recorded a 0.21% WoW rise, increasing from $58.02 to $58.14 this week, while all non-zero credit prices decreased by 3.72% WoW, from $65.58 to $63.14. Type 1 trades saw 315,374 credits exchanged, reflecting a fall of 12.20% WoW. The total transaction volume for all non-zero trades saw a fall of 11.30% to reach 2,226,528 credits. CA LCFS Daily Average Spot Price [caption id="attachment_595667" align="alignnone" width="1000"] Fig 2. California LCFS Weekly average spot prices and benchmark (*CC.Info OTC: Numbers are an average of broker prices reported to CC.info)[/caption] Last week, the average LCFS spot credit prices decreased by 1.39% WoW, reaching $58.13/MT. On 22nd April, the average spot price was $56.25/MT, while the benchmark, representing the future price of contracts expiring at the end of 2025, stood at $60.75/MT. CA LCFS Weekly Trade Frequency and Volume [caption id="attachment_595668" align="alignnone" width="1000"] Fig 3. Trade frequency and volume, Source: California ARB[/caption] As per the data reported by CARB last week, 107 trades were reported in the market, with 24 high-volume trades. The highest transfer by price was $79.00 for a Type 2 transaction at a volume of 20,000 credits. Last week, the lowest transfer by price was $50.50 per credit of Type 2, reported for transactions at volumes of 50,000 and 20,000 credits. The smallest volume of transfer was for 7 credits of the Type 2 category, undertaken for a price of $54.00. The largest volume transfers of 141,000 credits of the Type 2 category were undertaken for price of $66.22. CA LCFS Monthly Volumes and Price Trend [caption id="attachment_595669" align="alignnone" width="1000"] Fig 4. Total monthly volumes and associated prices, Source: California ARB[/caption] Average (Type 1) spot price reached $58.46 as of 20th April, moderately higher that the Average (Type 1) spot prices for March, which stood at $58.44. Trading volumes have already reached 7.53 MMT this month, recording the highest volume of trades for a month ever recorded in the Programâ€™s history. CA LCFS Weekly Volume Price Trend [caption id="attachment_595670" align="alignnone" width="1000"] Fig 5. Volume Price Trend indicator for the CA LCFS market, Source: California ARB[/caption] The Volume Price Trend Indicator (VPT) helps identify the parity between supply and demand for a stock. The idea behind the indicator is to multiply the market volume of LCFS credits by the percentage change in its price. If the credit price declines, the value of the indicator falls. Across 13th April â€“ 20th April, total VPT recorded a 17.18% fall WoW, with a fall in all non-zero prices. Renewable Fuel Standard (RFS): RIN weekly price trend RINs are credits used for compliance and are the â€œcurrencyâ€ of the RFS program. Renewable fuel producers generate RINs, market participants trade RINs, and obligated parties obtain and then ultimately retire RINs for the compliance list. D3 RIN prices witnessed a fall of 1.19% WoW. D4, D5 and D6 RIN prices increased by 1.04% WoW each. D3: Cellulosic biofuel D4: Biomass-based diesel D5: Advanced biofuels (produced from any type of renewable biomass except corn starch ethanol) D6: Conventional renewable biofuel ethanol derived from corn starch, or any other qualifying renewable fuel Upcoming dates Key Clean Fuels Market Developments this week Rhoden and Colleagues Urge EPA to Increase Renewable Fuel Levels Governor Larry Rhoden and three fellow governors wrote Environmental Protection Agency (EPA) Administrator Lee Zeldin, urging him to set higher Renewable Volume Obligation (RVO) levels. â€œOver the past several years, the biofuels industry has made significant investments to expand domestic production capacity and strengthen feedstock supply. Unfortunately, the Renewable Volume Obligation (RVO) levels set by the previous Administration failed to reflect this growth,â€ wroteÂ Governor Larry Rhoden and his colleagues. â€œAs a result, an increasing number of biofuel producersâ€¦have been forced to slow or cease operations, costing rural communities jobs and weakening key markets for American farmers.â€ Growth Energy, CFAA Respond to Oil Industry in Case Pushing EPA to Reallocate Lost Biofuel Gallons Growth Energy and Clean Fuels Alliance America (CFAA) filed a reply brief today in a case challenging the U.S. Environmental Protection Agency (EPA) for its failure to reallocate gallons lost due to small refinery exemptions (SREs) granted after renewable volume obligations (RVOs) have been issued under the Renewable Fuel Standard (RFS). Arkansas Legislators Pass Sustainable Aviation Fuel Incentive Act Arkansas lawmakers passed HB 1303, now Act 546 to create the Sustainable Aviation Fuel Incentive Act. The SAF faclity would need to have an installed facility cost of more than two 33 billion dollars ($2,000,000,000), begin construction on or before December 31, 2027, in addition to other criteria. The Act allows of an income tax credit of an amount equal to thirty percent (30%) of the cost of sustainable aviation fuel production and processing equipment purchased for use in the state by a qualified manufacturer. IMO Approves Net-zero Regulations for Global Shipping The International Maritime Organization (IMO) has approved draft regulations to reduce greenhouse gas (GHG) emissions from ships globally, aiming for net-zero emissions by or around, i.e close to 2050. It will be formally adopted in October before coming into force in 2027. Approved by the Marine Environment Protection Committee during its 83rd session (MEPC 83) from 7â€“11 April 2025, the measures include a new fuel standard for ships and a global pricing mechanism for emissions. USDA Announces Cancellation of the Partnerships for Climate-Smart Commodities (PCSC) " U.S. Secretary of Agriculture Brooke Rollins announced the cancellation of the Partnerships for Climate-Smart Commodities (PCSC). Select projects may continue if it is demonstrated that a significant amount of the federal funds awarded will go to farmers. Additionally, the USDA has reformed and overhauled the Biden-era Partnerships for Climate Smart Commodities initiative into the Advancing Markets for Producers (AMP) initiative, and has identified changes to align the initiative with current Trump Administration priorities. USDA will review existing grant agreements based on three Farmer First policy priorities: A minimum of 65% of federal funds must go to producers;Grant recipients must have enrolled at least one producer as of 12/31/2024; and Grant recipients must have made a payment to at least one producer as of 12/31/2024." Global Clean Energy Holdings and Certain Key Stakeholders Initiate Formal Pathway Aimed at Achieving a Sustainable Capital Structure Global Clean Energy Holdings, Inc. (OTCMKTS: GCEH) ("GCE" or the "Company"), a vertically integrated renewable fuels company, announced today it entered into a Restructuring Support Agreement (the â€œRSAâ€) with agreement and support from Vitol Americas Corp., as RCF lender, an ad hoc group of term loan lenders which holds approximately 96% of the term loans, and CTCI Americas, Inc. (â€œCTCIâ€). To facilitate the transactions contemplated under the RSA, the Company commenced Chapter 11 cases in the United States Bankruptcy Court for the Southern District of Texas. Valero Announces Notice to the California Energy Commission Regarding its Benicia, California, Refinery Valero Energy Corporation (NYSE: VLO, â€œValeroâ€) announced today that its subsidiary, Valero Refining Company-California, has submitted notice to the California Energy Commission of its current intent to idle, restructure, or cease refining operations at Valeroâ€™s Benicia Refinery by the end of April 2026. Valero continues to evaluate strategic alternatives for its remaining operations in California. â€œWe understand the impact that this may have on our employees, business partners, and community, and will continue to work with them through this period,â€ said Lane Riggs, Chairman, CEO and President of Valero. Washington State Senate Passes HB 1409 on Clean Fuel Standard On April 15, Washington State Legislature passed HB 1409 on the Clean Fuel Standard. The Act steepens the carbon intensity requirements for transportation fuels under the Clean Fuels Program (CFP). It further removes violations of CFP from civil and criminal penalty statutes in the Washington Clean Air Act and instead, establishes CFP-specific enforcement and penalties.
